data.title: JPIE: Continues To Impress And Gather Assets
data.description:
Thesis

We have not written about the JPMorgan Income ETF (JPIE) for almost a year now. Last time we covered the fund we highlighted its resilience during the April 2025 market downturn, and its continued outperformance. Since our last piece the fund has continued to add assets, becoming a massive $7 billion plus ETF:

Please note the exponential explosion in AUM after April 2025, with assets more than doubling since.

In today's piece we are going to revisit the name and its holdings, and outline why we still believe JPIE to be a solid holding in today's macro environment.

Current Composition

The fund continues to position itself very smartly, away from overextended asset classes:

Agency MBS bonds have the highest representation in the portfolio at 34.5%, followed by cash at 14% and CMBS at 11.6%. Corporate high yield, a sector we consider overextended, is at just 11%. Similarly, the fund presents a table which shows the contributors to its duration profile. Again, here we are seeing the correct strategy taken - duration is taken for the non-stretched asset classes:

Agency MBS is the largest contributor here, followed by EM bonds. Please note we are using a fungible duration profile here - considering spread and credit duration to be very similar for the fund (spread duration means sensitivity to interest rates, while credit spread duration means sensitivity to credit spread moves).

The ETF is keeping a very high-quality holdings' composition, with a significant AAA sectoral allocation:

  • AAA: 55%.
  • AA: 2.6%.
  • A: 4%.
  • BBB: 9.2%.
  • BB: 13.2%.
  • B: 4.5%.
  • Not rated: 10.8%.
  • Other: residual.

In the current set-up, the fund is taking very little credit risk, with only 18% of the holdings being sub investment grade. The ETF is an active one, however, so the fund composition can change as the managers see fit. The fund's trailing 12-months turnover ratio is 120%, so this name changes its holdings a lot.

Further fund analytics are as follows:

  • Expense ratio: 0.39%.
  • 30-day SEC yield: 5.64%.
  • Dividend frequency: monthly.
  • Duration: 2.15 years.

The fund has a fairly elevated expense ratio, but this is to be expected for an active ETF. The name has delivered, and we are not bothered by the expense ratio.

Solid Performance In A Decreasing Rate Environment

Keep in mind that the larger the duration the better when rates are coming down. JPIE has a very short duration, but has managed to post very robust total returns:

We are using a total return measurement here in order to include dividends, and a cohort formed by JPIE, the iShares Flexible Income ETF (BINC), the PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Fund (MINT), the iShares Short Duration Bond ETF (NEAR) and the iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV).

BINC is the outperformer from the cohort, closely followed by JPIE. Both names posted total returns around 7% in the past year. Kindly note that the main portfolio manager for BINC is Rick Rieder, whom is now favored by the prediction markets to be the next Fed Chair.

What is also very important for JPIE and its holders is the low volatility associated with the name. A fund that produces 7% total returns with a 15% volatility is not as appealing as one doing that with a 1.98% annualized volatility:

As we highlighted in the holdings section, the ETF is very smart when it comes to composition. The fund only buys assets which tend to be undervalued, while keeping overextended assets with very short duration profiles (i.e. U.S. high yield). This ensures any drawdowns are very shallow. We saw that in April 2025:

The fund had a drawdown of roughly -2% during April 2025, drawdown which was closed-out within a month. The drawdown is very much in line with the volatility figures computed by the Seeking Alpha platform.

This is exactly what you want to see for a fund like JPIE - decent total returns with very low volatility figures. Market crashes will happen, and there is no way to avoid them, but positioning a fund smartly ensures the drawdowns are shallow and resolved quickly.

Investors need to be picky in today's environment - we recently gave a 'Sell' rating to an HY CEF which has 30% leverage and the following total return in the past year:

The annualized volatility can be seen with a naked eye here for the BlackRock Multi-Sector Income Trust (BIT).

We Favor Smart Risk

In today's macro environment with tight credit spreads we favor smart risk taking. As we saw above with BIT, buying a CEF with a high stated distribution rate does not equate a high total return. It is ok to buy a name like JPIE with a low stated distribution but an active trading profile and proved total returns. When the next market downturn comes, one can just add smart risk given the low drawdown profile. We can see from the chart above how BIT had a -10% drawdown in April 2025.

JPIE might not be exciting when looking at the yield figures, but the fund represents a robust portfolio holding with a low volatility and an impressive total return potential in an uncertain environment.

Conclusion

JPIE is a fixed income exchange-traded fund from the JPMorgan platform. The name has posted a total return of almost 7% in the past year, with a very low volatility profile. We like the fund composition and its duration profile, and find this active ETF ideal for today's macro. The market seems to agree, with AUM more than doubling in the past year, now sitting just north of $7 billion.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Big Name Earnings
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Large cap tech earnings (0:25) Health insurance stocks drop (6:55) Good news in travel and leisure (8:30) More big earnings next week (10:00) Interesting new Fed Chair (12:20) Cloudy macro data (15:30)

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, Seeking Alpha's stalwart Director of News. The last episode of January. It's a doozy, we got a lot to talk about. Welcome back to the show. Talk to us. Earnings, new Fed chair, brouhaha galore.

Brian Stewart: Yeah, there's a lot going on. Thank you very much.

Let's jump in. Yeah, I think jumping on earnings, right? So we had a bunch of big name earnings with Microsoft (MSFT). We had Meta (META), Apple (AAPL), Tesla (TSLA) this past week, kind of the meat of the earnings season getting going.

I think it's interesting to look at Microsoft and Meta as a pair. Both companies beat expectations. Both companies revealed higher CapEx spending for Meta. They're now spending $17 billion in the past quarter for R&D. That's 41 % growth from last year. It was 15 billion in Q3, was 13 billion in Q2.

Back when Meta changed its name from Facebook to Meta in like that specific quarter, they had spent 6 billion on R&D. So you can just see them constantly cranking up the amount they're spending.

Meanwhile, the stocks, Microsoft and Meta went in different directions. So Microsoft was down 10%. Meta was up about 10%. And so there's an interesting dichotomy and how the market was reacting to what seems on the surface is relatively similar news.

I think one thing to look at is just the relative placement that Microsoft stock and Meta stock had going into the earnings report. So as of the end of 2025, Microsoft was up 17% over a one year basis and Meta was down 4%. So you had more of a optimistic view of Microsoft's prospects as we were closing out 2025.

And then going into the earnings report, you saw Meta rise five out of the six sessions headed into the earnings report. And you see it pop with the earnings coming out. And so I just think you're seeing Microsoft and Meta converging.

You're seeing the market pricing them sort of similarly in terms of where they are in the upgrade cycle. I think you're also seeing a little bit more optimism about the kind of blocking and tackling that Meta is doing versus Microsoft, just sort of the core business as opposed to the projections for AI going forward.

So you see in Meta earnings report, it's basic figures, so average revenue per person was $16.56 for the fourth quarter that's up 16 % from last year. They've now had 10 straight quarters of double digit growth in that area. That figure was at $9.57 in Q1 of 2022. So you're seeing it rise pretty strongly over the last several years.

Meanwhile at Microsoft, they're still having solid growth in their cloud space, which has been the driver recently that that seems like it might be starting to hit a peak. So there's a little bit worry at Microsoft that they're reaching their ceiling there where at Meta, I think the idea is they're doing a great job executing on their core business, and they also have this investment piece, this AI piece that that is on the horizon still.

So I think just in the place where the stocks were, I think you're just seeing a slight recalibration of people's expectations.

Just because they're big names, I think we should jump into Tesla and Apple. Tesla was down a little on its earnings report initially popped and then kind of faded as the day went on. The stock in general is hovering off its highs.

As a car company, this is the story of of Tesla over the last several years. So as a car company, it's pretty meh, right? It's a niche car producer. It's nowhere near somebody like a Ford (F) or (GM). It's being passed by some of its EV rivals in terms of deliveries. Both deliveries of production were down in the fourth quarter.

But in terms of the stock price, in terms of your average Tesla investor, and certainly in terms of Elon Musk's vision for the company, it's presented more as a technology company, broadly speaking. And so this earnings report had that story embedded in it.

Like I said, you have the declines in terms of deliveries and production. But you also see the company making real material shifts towards some of the other products that they've been touting as being on the horizon for a while.

So you're seeing the company halt model S, X production, give that manufacturing capacity to the Optimus human robot. You're also seeing the company highlight progress that it's making on robot taxis and proprietary AI chips. So you're continuing to see the company present itself as more than, like the car is just the way to process experiments that they're going to do a broader sort of technological revolution.

I think in general, investors are still on board with that message. I think in the wake of this particular earnings report, they just sort of figured wait and see, we'll check back in when we get some real robot production, when we get some real robot taxi progress.

And then just turning to Apple, Apple was also down slightly after its earnings report. was a blockbuster quarter in terms of its sales. iPhone revenue topped $85 billion. Services revenue topped $30 billion. Those are both quarterly revenue records for those segments of the company.

I think the problem with Apple is that people don't see the same kind of investment they're seeing in places like Microsoft and Meta. Apple's being much quieter about the progress it's making on the AI front.

It's sort of just going about its business, selling iPhones and having kind of bubbling in the background, the idea that it's going to have these other technological jumps in the future.

I think the market is again in a wait and see attitude with Apple thinking at some point we're going to see what they have behind the curtain.

Rena Sherbill: And what else would you put in the stock specific conversation?

Brian Stewart: Just a couple of stories that might've flown a little under the radar, given the high profile earnings reports.

We had UnitedHealth (UNH) drop sharply. It had earnings and that included negative guidance. It projected a revenue decline in 2026, which is the first time in decades the company has seen a revenue decline. The real driver for the stock drop, the stock drops about 20% after its earnings report, was the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services proposed a very tiny fractional increase in the payment rates for 2027.

So this impacted United Health, which has a huge managed care portion of its business. And then other health insurance stocks dropped as well. So Humana (HUM) was down 21%. (CVS), which owns Aetna was down 14%. Molina Healthcare (MOH) was down 8%.

So basically investors in this space got the equivalent of a tonsillectomy without anesthesia. Just 20% of the stuff taken off the top, maybe something worse than tonsillectomy. The problem with this in terms of these investors is this is a long-term situation. It can't be just framed as one bad quarter because these are the rates that are upcoming for the next year. So it's really a political story to the extent that this industry can push for higher rates.

And then turning to another sort of more positive story. Royal Caribbean (RCL) rose 19 % after its earnings, stronger than expected guidance, including double digit growth in both revenue and earnings. It touted strong early bookings.

Elsewhere, Southwest (LUV) also up 19 % after its earnings. Another strong forecast. The company is projecting EPS to rise 300 % in 2026 versus 2025.

A lot of that is specific to Southwest. They're going through a transition now where they're kind of moving more in lockstep with the rest of the airline industry. So they're getting more revenue from things like baggage charges and charging for more legroom on planes. Basically going to that model where you buy your ticket, but then you have to pay for every sort of little upgrade that you want.

But in terms of the profitability that the company is pursuing for 2026, it seems to be working. And just taking the two companies in general, we're only talking to stocks and so it can't be seen as a harbinger for the entire travel and leisure industry.

But just in terms of like economic strength, if people are buying cruises or people are getting on planes and traveling. It shows that there's underlying demand for that kind of stuff.

Rena Sherbill: And then when it comes to earnings next week, what are you looking forward to?

Brian Stewart: The big ones, Amazon (AMZN) on Thursday, Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) is on Wednesday.

Amazon just announced another round of layoffs, getting rid of some corporate jobs, replacing basically those with AI. So I think more color on that with their plans going forward. There had been talk earlier about Amazon moving away from its partnership with the U S postal service, doing more of deliveries on its own, sort of building out that fleet, so maybe more on that.

Obviously AWS is going to be the key driver, that's what people are going to dig in for that one. So I think it'll be interesting as a tech play just in the AWS part of it.

I also think Amazon's also interesting just in the economic front, even if you're not an Amazon shareholder, I think their earnings speak a lot about the consumer and what's going on there. And then, like I said before, there's a couple of inner company things that might be interesting to get updates on.

And then Alphabet, I think, falls into the same category as the Microsofts, the Metas, the Apples of the world. Big tech players, how well are they executing on their their current business and what are their plans for AI in the future?

I think there's going to be a lot of looking at Alphabet spending plans. Are they sort of in the the Meta camp where no price is too high? Or are they being more judicious about their investments? More kind of an Apple model there.

And then just among other notable stocks, you got Uber (UBER), you've got Qualcomm (QCOM), and then a bunch of pharmaceutical stocks are coming out. Merck (MRK), Lilly (LLY), Pfizer (PFE). So keep an eye on those as well.

Rena Sherbill: Yes, lots of clues and outright facts to be picked up along the way as we try and understand the trajectory of these sectors and how they're gonna grow and evolve.

Brian Stewart: Yeah. I was going to say, on the leisure front to Disney's (DIS) coming up. So if you're following up on the Southwest Royal Caribbean piece of the market, I think Disney and their theme parks and that kind of thing, I think would be very useful.

Rena Sherbill: Yes, absolutely.

When it comes to macro news, we had our illustrious host Julie Morgan talking to us that Kevin Warsh was probably going to be picked in the later hours of the morning and Kim Kahn just released a fantastic special episode about Mr. Warsh's appointment. What would you add to this conversation about our new Fed chair in America?

Brian Stewart: Yeah, it's hard for me to say more than than Kim would. I would point listeners to to check out that for sure. I think. The most interesting part of this is well, for for one thing, with Trump nominating his own person to succeed Powell, you're going to see the political temperature turn down a little bit and.

But I do think that there's going to be concerns about the independence of the Fed in the sense that Powell was extremely, he drew a very bright line. He was not going to be influenced. He was not going to be bullied. He was going to do what he thought was right.

And I think a lot of investors and a lot of market watchers had issues with what Powell thought was right. I think a lot of people thought he was late in the post pandemic environment, let inflation get out of control a little bit and then wasn't able to tamp it down fast enough. And we're still sort of living with the aftermath of that.

So I think there's a lot of criticism that can be laid at Powell. But the one thing that he was unbending about was the bright line between the Fed and the sort of political parts of the government. With Warsh owing his appointment to Trump, I think it becomes we'll have to see what he says.

We, I think as investors, him holding that line would be really important. The other thing about Warsh that I find interesting is it's a little hard to kind of see where his head is at. When he was on the Fed previously, he was seen as a hawk. He kind of clashed openly with Ben Bernanke's easy money policies in the wake of the great recession and the financial crisis.

But lately, he's been talking more about the disinflationary impact of technology, basically suggesting that the Fed can can let things run a little more, be less strict in their view on inflation because technology and the markets in general are going to kind of take care of some of that as well. So it it's interesting.

I mean, his service on the Fed was more than a decade ago now. So the idea that his ideas might have shifted from that point isn't unusual, but it'll be, I think people are going to be looking closely, especially during his confirmation hearings and things like that, exactly where he is in terms of the hawkish dovish divide.

Rena Sherbill: Yes, yes, so much to pay attention to.

What would you say about the data we're seeing from the macro perspective?

Brian Stewart: So next week's a big one. Next week's the latest jobs data. The previous jobs data was pretty underwhelming. 50,000 jobs added during the month, which is pretty anemic.

I'm not making a prediction, but it's highly possible that we could see that dip into the negative with a revision. could also be revised higher. So we'll see what happens with revisions. It's been pretty common over I'd say the last like year 18 months to see previous months revised lower. So keep an eye on that.

And then the unemployment rate had risen and then dipped again, dipped back in the latest is now sitting at 4.4%, which is fine. It's well in the fine range. But the direction that that's taking, like, is it going to drift lower again? Or, you know, we've been getting anecdotal news of layoffs and things like that. Amazon, the one that I mentioned earlier. So see how much of that is sort of playing a part in the overall economy.

Rena Sherbill: Anything you would say about the consumer confidence report that was released this week that it was the lowest since the pandemic?

Brian Stewart: I have to admit that the economic situation is hard to parse. It seems real cloudy to me.

Rena Sherbill: Cloudy with a chance of partisanship?

Brian Stewart: Yeah, 100 percent. There's a lot of articles - if you just scroll, you see the headlines of, why does the economy feel so bad if it's not so bad kind of angles on it?

And I think there is sort of a feeling of because a lot of the harshness in the economy now is in on the inflation front, the prices are still rising higher than the Fed target. I mean, it's down from where it was, but we're still seeing higher prices.

And obviously the higher prices that came into effect over the past few years are still there. You get sticker shock just going to McDonald's. And I think people live that day to day, right?

And so I think it's just sort of like walking through a hailstorm, you know what I mean? It's little things on you, every step you take. And I think that makes consumers feel the economy more constantly.

The main problem, the economy is a lack of jobs and the unemployment rate is going up and, you know, you see negatives for the monthly employment rate. You either have a job or you don't. So if you're affected by the bad economy, you lose your job, that's horrific, right? But most people aren't gonna be affected. Even in a recession, most people are going to keep their jobs.

So you're sort of affected personally by seeing people around you getting affected. Whereas the higher prices kind of affects everybody a little bit all the time. So I think in terms of consumer confidence, that might play a part in, it just feels like a slog day to day, which is why I think affordability has become sort of the watchword politically.

And then, we have the midterms coming. So your point about partisanship being critical to the way people are metabolizing the economy. I think that's true because I think that you're going to see that debate ratched up. You'll see politicians telling consumers constantly over the next few months that affordability is a problem. So I think that just sort of reinforces the feeling that consumers already have.

Rena Sherbill: If it walks like inflation and feels like inflation, is it in fact good news? Any final words for this week, Brian?

Brian Stewart: Earnings keep rolling on. So I think that'll be the watchword for next week, though it's been a situation in the last, through Trump's term so far is you kind of never know if there's something going to be like a Venezuela situation, a Greenland situation. We almost had a partial shutdown of the government over the clashes in in Minneapolis. The things that feel separate from markets, separate from the economy can kind of steer into the economy.

I think the investors have gotten used to this. You have to be a little bit more ready to take in the news and do what's appropriate.

Rena Sherbill: We've got to be nimble.

Brian Stewart: Right, nimble, that's the word I was looking for, that's perfect.

Rena Sherbill: To nimbleness. Talk to you next week.

Brian Stewart: Sounds good. Talk to you next week.

data.title: BJ's Wholesale Club: Solid Expansion, But Valuation Leaves Little Room For Error
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

BJ's Wholesale Club (BJ) has been performing well in recent years, reducing their debt and currently planning to expand significantly, but I believe the current valuation leaves little room for error, while the environment is facing pressure from several angles.

BJ’s Q3 was solid overall, beating the market’s estimates and updating their FY 2025 outlook, expecting Adjusted EPS between $4.30 and $4.40 (vs. $4.20 to $4.35) and comparable club sales (ex-gasoline) up 2% to 3%.

The company’s free cash flow fell over the 9M’25 period to $138.15 million compared to compared to $201.40 million over 9M’24, although most of that came as a result of the higher CAPEX, as they are investing in aggressively expanding their club footprint, with a very solid real estate pipeline while also developing the gas station additions to existing clubs and developing their digital presence and supply chain.

Also important to note is that their cash flow tends to be seasonal, with Q4 being a major contributor to last year’s free cash flow of $312.89 million in 2024, while reporting $251.81 million in 2023 and $390.36 million in 2022.

However, BJ’s latest guidance sees CAPEX at ~$800 million in 2025, that means they are going to spend roughly $300 million in Q4, dragging the free cash flow down significantly, although they could still deliver at least $100 million to even $200 million in 2025 despite the massive CAPEX jump ($100 million would basically imply zero growth in net cash from operations compared to 2024, which is unlikely given the investments made).

Especially since they have the current two-year aggressive expansion plan to open 25 to 30 new clubs (in 2025 and 2026), we can likely see even higher CAPEX in 2026, after which the free cash flow can increase significantly as CAPEX would drop and they'd reap the benefits of these accelerated investments.

Financially, based on BJ’s latest report, we can see a solid position, even though the current assets aren’t covering their current liabilities and they have a limited amount of cash on hand. The company barely has long-term debt (reduced debt by over $2.1 billion since FY17), which can pose an advantage that should allow them to withstand potential macro issues for longer.

Regarding shareholder returns, although BJ doesn’t pay a dividend, they do buybacks, with $135 million spent on repurchases over the 9M'25 period, while they still had ~$866.2 million available under their 2024 Repurchase Program, which expired in January 2029. Annualized, that’s about $180 million, meaning a buyback yield of about 1.5%.

BJ’s Q4 Earnings report is expected to be released on March 5, and I expect this to come with higher volatility since the company is likely going to at least hint at their expectations for 2026, which often comes with more importance than an earnings beat or miss.

Given the seasonal nature of the cash flow, I will pay close attention to that number and expect the quarter to account for a big chunk of the year’s FCF. I’ll also pay attention to their views on the environment, as they have access to key data about an important category of the economy.

Risks & Opportunities

Despite an overall solid performance, BJ ultimately relies a lot on the economy, as their clients are exposed significantly to the rising debt costs and lower consumer discretionary budgets that come as a result of the Fed’s previous rate hikes, which were meant to slow down the economy to fight off inflation.

However, there is the risk to see this “organic” weakness be amplified by factors such as tariffs, the rising fear around the Fed’s independence once Powell’s term ends in May 2026, rising national debt, as well as the falling USD, which recently got into the spotlight.

All of these can have significant effects that go beyond the Fed’s ability, as the market can continue to demand high or even higher yields for longer, regardless of whether the rate cuts continue - and we can already see that to some degree, as treasury yields are back to some of the highest levels since August 2025, basically ignoring the rate cuts that came later in 2025.

This is the market’s way to adjust the reward to the new risks it perceives, but we also have worries around the situation in Japan recently pushing the dollar even lower, while the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget (CRFB) has warned that the rate at which the national debt grows could soon outpace the US economy, saying it could lead to “six distinct types of fiscal crises.”

Meanwhile, the “rate check” combined with the worries around the Japanese Yen led to even more money fleeing into precious metals, with gold briefly breaching a monumental $5,600 per ounce before retreating.

Simply put, Japan is the largest foreign owner of US treasuries, and if they sell these to consolidate their currency, it can put pressure on the US at a terrible time (potentially lifting the yield even more), while an intervention that could imply selling dollars to buy JPY (which I’ll add that US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent ruled out “absolutely”) can be just as bad, essentially “locking” the market in a very complicated position.

Also, the fact that the company is developing like this despite the current consumer pressure is quite impressive, as the weakness felt by the final consumer can clearly affect the company’s clients. The company’s management also commented on the current environment during their Q3 Earnings Call, stating:

With regards to guidance and as we have been speaking to on this call, the macro environment is challenging. We have made decisions to be prudent with inventories in the face of this environment, challenging our ability to grow general merchandise sales. We made that choice in order to allow continued investment in member value in the rest of the business. While it will hamper sales in the short term, we remain confident that this was the right decision.

The company’s size can also be an advantage, as they stand at a sweet spot that could allow them to capitalize on smaller opportunities while also enjoying plenty of room to grow - or even represent an acquisition target for a giant that wants to consolidate and integrate their assets into their own network to deliver synergies.

Risks Reiterated

BJ will ultimately depend on the economy as a proxy for its clients, which can certainly affect their growth and profitability, potentially lowering the return on the current investment cycle and disappointing the market’s expectations.

Meanwhile, the environment certainly has room to get worse given all the factors that are developing, which could even lead to them reconsidering their investment plans and going into a more defensive mode instead, while the uncertainty around the USD and US equities can also tax the company’s valuation.

Valuation

For BJ's DCF to equity valuation, I'll go for the midpoint of my estimated free cash flow mentioned above, meaning $150 million as a base in 2025 and 2026 to account for their higher CAPEX. From 2027, I'll assume that the CAPEX drops to $500 million, which should be enough to sustain a solid level of growth while also normalizing, potentially getting us to about $600 million (basically assuming a CAGR of a bit less than 7.5% for the cash flow from operations compared to 2024’s ~$900 million), which is then projected to grow at a CAGR of 6% until year 5 (similar to the market’s size CAGR since 2007) as they can continue developing while the environment could improve, followed by a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, as well as an 8% discount rate and 131.92 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $11.82 billion, for an intrinsic value of $89.60, which is nearly identical to the current $90.50 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

I rate BJ a Hold, as even though the company is solid overall and has potential, especially following the current investment cycle, the price already reflects a lot of it. Especially given the potential issues and pressure in the environment if things get worse, the overall risk-reward doesn’t seem worth it at this point - speaking from a value point of view.

data.title: SLC Agrícola Looks Undervalued, But Current Trends Aren't Helpful
data.description:
Good management matters, particularly over the long term, but there are limits to what management can do, and that’s particularly true in agriculture, where weather, global market trends, and geopolitics can have major impacts on crop prices. I continue to believe that SLC Agrícola (SLCJY) (SLCJF) is one of the best-run ag companies and is positioned to generate value for shareholders over time, but it’s tough to find a lot of good news in the near term.

It’s been quite a while since I’ve written on SLC Agrícola, and the shares have seen some significant swings over that time in response to commodity prices, yields, and sentiment around global trade. Frustratingly, while management has exceeded expectations (mine and the Street’s) in those years since that last article, near-term commodity prices tend to dominate the trading on these shares, so ongoing growth in yields and acres under cultivation hasn't really translated into reliable gains.

I do think SLC Agrícola is undervalued today, and more challenging weather and operating conditions in Brazil could, perhaps counterintuitively, work to SLC Agrícola’s benefit over time. That said, this crop year is shaping up as relatively lackluster in terms of yield growth and margins, so while I do think the shares are undervalued, it’s difficult to find a clear turning point for sentiment right now.

A Tougher Year For Brazil

While Brazil has often been characterized as a net winner in the ongoing trade disputes between the United States and China, with the latter willing and able to buy soybeans and other crops from Brazil as an alternative to U.S. exports, the on-the-ground situation is more complicated and not particularly good at present.

Although planted acreage has grown for the ‘25/’26 crop season, the rate of growth has slowed noticeably, with ‘25/’26 acreage up low single digits compared to a trailing 20-year average growth rate closer to 6%. Not only have commodity prices weakened (soy is closer to its five-year low than mean) and input prices increased, but credit availability has declined, with farmers facing higher rates and tighter lending standards.

Moreover, there are uncertainties around weather and geopolitics. At present it looks as though Brazil’s prime growing regions are going to see hotter, dryer weather, and that’s bad for yields, particularly in the context of fewer productivity inputs. There’s also uncertainty on the trade front, with some moderate thawing between the U.S. and China and the possibility that China may return to buying U.S. soybeans, at least on a limited basis.

These drivers have mixed direct relevance to SLC Agrícola, and the longer-term impact could be a little more nuanced.

In the near term, weaker commodity prices are an issue. As of the late update (Q3’25), SLC Agrícola was about 60% hedged for its soy crop around $11/bu, and current prices are a little lower than that. Corn and cotton are likewise not in great shape, with cotton prices near a five-year low (and cotton has historically been SLC Agrícola’s most lucrative crop per hectare).

Input costs are likewise a challenge in the near term, with management expecting double-digit cost increases across its crop portfolio. The combination of yield improvement and planting mix is not likely to be all that helpful either, with corn yields likely to retreat, soy up modestly, and cotton more mixed (good for the first crop, bad for the second).

Longer term, these trends may not be so bad for SLC Agrícola. As one of the largest players, SLC Agrícola has staying power that others do not, including reliable access to credit and capital markets. The company isn’t dependent on bank financing to plant its acreage, and the company has the resources to continue investing in value-added inputs like fertilizer and irrigation. Moreover, pressure on the sector can shake out weaker hands, allowing SLC Agrícola to add to its land bank on an opportunistic basis.

Core Competencies Matter And Are Very Much Intact

It’s been a while since I’ve written about SLC Agrícola, so I think it’s worth refreshing some of what I think are the core points and positive aspects of this company.

For starters, management has steadily added to its acreage, growing its planted acreage from 377kha in 2015/2016 to 463kha in 2020/21 to 830kha in 2025/26, while pursuing a strategy of roughly one-third owned land and two-thirds leased land. Management has also steadily improved the quality of its land, investing in improvements like irrigation and making consistent use of yield-enhancing inputs and technology like precision agriculture and regenerative agriculture.

These investments continue to translate into impressive yields. While overall soybean yields in Brazil have improved significantly with the adoption of modern farming technologies (from around 2,902kg/ha a decade ago to 3,500kg/ha), SLC Agrícola has lifted its yield to nearly 4,000kg/ha—better than the U.S. average (around 3,600kg/ha) and almost on par with the best yields in the U.S. (4,250kg/ha).

The same is largely true with cotton. SLC Agrícola’s first crop cotton yield was 2,034kg/ha last year, up from around 1,600kg/ha a decade ago, and comfortably above the Brazilian average (1,880kg/ha) and U.S. average around 935kg/ha, and also competitive with China's 2,200kg/ha or so average (which has improved markedly over the last decade).

With prudent use of hedges, SLC Agrícola has managed its exposures well, and the company has reliably generated EBITDAs in the low-30%s (on an adjusted basis), with the best years in the high-30%s to low-40%s and the worst year of the last decade at about 15%. Land acquisitions have created volatility in the free cash flow numbers, but only one of the last 10 years has been negative, and “core” FCF margin (that excludes acquisitions) has averaged more than 20%.

SLC Agrícola has also seen steady growth in its land valuation. The most recent land bank valuation was up almost 16% from the prior year (to R$13.4 billion), and if you look only at the land that SLC Agrícola owned back in 2007, valuation has grown at an annualized rate of over 13%. It’s questionable just how impactful these valuations are, given that the shares have virtually always traded at a discount to the NAV implied by these assessments and land transactions often occur at meaningful discounts to these valuations, but I think the consistent growth in assessed value is still meaningful.

The Outlook

It’ll be a while before we see fourth-quarter results (March 14), and the company will most likely see high single-digit declines in revenue and EBITDA when it does report (around 8%-9%), with an EBITDA margin around 30%. Hopefully there’s good news about yields, but that’s up to the weather, and I doubt there will be significant relief on costs. Likewise, given how commodities have been trading, I don’t think the hedging activities will be particularly exciting.

Modeling an agriculture company can feel fatuous at times, as so much depends upon weather, global prices, trade flows, and so on. That said, SLC Agrícola has been pretty reliable when it comes to growing its acreage, improving its yields, and driving 30%-ish EBITDA margins.

To that end, I’m somewhat confident that the company will continue to generate “as is” revenue growth of around 6% over time from ongoing yield improvements and crop price recoveries. Actual revenue will likely be higher, given further land acquisition, but I’m not modeling that. I do expect EBITDA margins will be below-trend for FY’26 and likely FY’27 given input price inflation and a glut of key crops, but I don’t see why the underlying profitability should change much over time (long-term EBITDA margins in the 32%-33% range).

Discounted cash flow suggests a fair value for the ADRs of around $4/share, and a 6x multiple on my ’26 EBITDA estimate gets me to $4.25/share. That’s below the long-term average multiple (closer to 6.5x), but I think some degree of discount is appropriate given the more challenging near-term conditions.

Speaking practically, though, it’s going to be commodity prices that ultimately have the most impact on the share price, and that’s a factor that investors just have to live with if they want to own SLC Agrícola. The following charts hopefully illustrate this point a little better.

The Bottom Line

If you think row crop prices should improve, SLC Agrícola could be an interesting name. Likewise, if you think the market will, over time, reward the company for its operating quality and land value growth, I think today’s price is appealing. While I do think these shares should trade higher, investors need to understand the above-average volatility and risk here, and these shares certainly aren’t for everyone.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Intel: The Real Catalyst Could Be Nvidia For 18A/14A
data.description:
Intel Corporation (INTC) has gained 25.6% since my last report, sharply outperforming the S&P 500’s 1.8% return. There has been a lot of optimism driven by investments from Nvidia (NVDA) and the U.S. Government. While I certainly believe that especially the partnership with Nvidia could be a bright spot, I do believe that the run-up in the stock price did not accurately depict that much of the real change at Intel is currently incremental, with potential bigger positive changes towards the end of the decade as more companies consider Intel Foundry for chip manufacturing.

At the same time, we note that she share price performance has brought Intel better in line with a peer group valuation. In this report, I discuss the company’s Q4 2025 earnings and reassess my rating and price target.

Intel Continues Streak of Conservative Guidance

Intel reported $13.7 billion in revenues, which marked a 4.1% year-on-year decline. Gross margins of $37.9 billion were down 4.2 points and earnings per share of $0.15 were up $0.02 year-on-year. What we continue to see with Intel is that the company is actually beating its quarterly guidance. Sales came in $0.4 billion above expectations while margins were around 1.4 points better than expected and EPS came in $0.07 higher than expected. You could see it as a sign of strength, but it is increasingly looking like Intel provided guidance that is too conservative. It is not necessarily a bad thing, but it also does not really reflect certainty on the outlook. I believe the company guides conservatively for the simple reason that any miss in estimates could immediately sour investor sentiment.

A Deeper Look at Intel Earnings

Intel Products sales declined 2.3% year-on-year to $12.9 billion. Sales in the Client Computing Group, or CCG, declined from $8.8 billion to $8.2 billion. Generally there is strength in demand for AI PC units, but sales and margins came down due to intel focusing internal wafer supply on the data center segment and outsources other work for CCG. That pressured margins, and the company also had an unfavorable mix. Furthermore, the Panther Lake is ramping up, which at this point still is a margin headwind. We also note that supply for DRAM and NAND is constrained, which also does not help the Client Computing Group results.

So, in CCG we didn’t see weak demand but constrained supply, prioritization, mix shifts and ramp up effects.

The Data Center and AI segment saw revenues increase by 7% year-on-year and 15% sequentially to $4.17 billion. Gross margins improved from 8.6% last year and 23.4% in Q3 to 26.4%. Driven by high demand for AI, we are also seeing demand for CPUS. Supply was the limiting factor on the revenue performance.

Intel Foundry saw revenues increase 6.4% sequentially to $4.2 billion, and loss margins widened to 545.7%. Around $222 million in revenues came from external customers. Margins were diluted by the ramp-up of 18A.

Intel Guidance Points at Declines

For the first quarter, Intel is guiding for $11.7-$12.7 billion in sales, indicating $0.5 billion lower sales year-on-year with margins down 4.7 points to 34.5%, with break-even EPS expected, which is down $0.13. I believe supply chain challenges remain a drag on the company’s CCG and DCAI performance. Foundry could benefit from packaging demand, but as 18A yields are not up-to-par yet, there will be substantial margin pressure.

Nvidia Could Be the 2028 Bright Spot

For 18A, Intel is not having a big lineup of external customers. Microsoft is said to be among the prospective customers using the 18A node technology. However, it does seem that 18A for now will be largely a node for internal use for Intel as prospective customers for Intel Foundry watch the ramp up and yields for 18A, potentially offload simpler tasks to Intel, and, based on that, could ultimately decide to utilize Intel Foundry for 14A, which would be a 2028-2029 opportunity. Prospective customers include Nvidia and Apple, as trade tension is forcing more domestic production.

Intel Stock Remains Risky, but I Am Upgrading

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Intel, I am upgrading the stock from Hold to Buy. The path remains risky as we see that for 2026 the downside is still substantially larger than the upside when we compare the stock price targets for the company median EV/EBITDA and the peer group. However, what we also see is that by 2028 that is completely reversed. On financial performance, I expect 5.5% annual growth in sales, 22% annual growth in EBITDA and free cash flow to become positive in 2027. As a result, I am upgrading the stock from Hold to Buy with a $66.52 price target.

Margins are expected to continue improving in the years ahead, driven by improving yields on 18A. Capital expenditures are currently expected to remain at a lower level, which should help the company return to positive free cash flow by 2027 and end up in a net cash position. Net debt leverage already dropped into the -1x-2x range, with further improvements expected in the years ahead. The years ahead are not business as usual and still show high capital intensity as the turnaround is still ongoing and operating cash flows and margins likely still have to reach a saturation point, but we are seeing strong signs of expected improvement.

Conclusion: Intel Stock Could Be a High Risk Buy

Intel still has a lot of work to do in the years ahead, and this year will be an extremely important one as the company ramps up Panther Lake and improves yield on 18A. I believe that yield improvements should ultimately help Intel secure key customers for 18A and later on 14A. I am upgrading INTC stock ahead of those decisions, as I believe that firm agreements for either 18A or 14A could be potential catalysts for Intel’s stock price.

data.title: Partners Group: Hitting Performance Fee Targets Seems Likely
data.description:
Partners Group Holding AG (PGPHF) recently delivered some of its 2026 outlook and a business update which does highlight a couple of strong points for the company. The first is that realisations are now being driven by equity as opposed to lower IRR categories like credit, which supports the outlook of 25%+ of revenue to come from performance fees. This is a change from our previous coverage. While PE is coming back online gradually across the board, Partners Group seems to be ahead of the industry both in realisations and therefore also in fundraising, as returned capital gets added to and redeployed. This puts them in a favourable competitive position in finding deals. The quarter demonstrates that Partners Group is well positioned with respect to key quality considerations in their portfolio, which is likely the dimension along which realisation is bifurcating in the industry.

Latest Disclosures

The first thing to highlights is record fundraising. The industry is not back at pre-2022 levels so this is them getting ahead of the curve.

We had $26 billion in fundraising during 2025, which represents our highest year for new client demand in our 30-year history.

Q4 Guidance Call

Realisations have been making raising new capital possible most likely, with less needed reliance on secondaries which for many others has been a key way for LPs to get liquidity. The realisations are now being concentrated in private equity, which is a good improvements from the last quarter as this was the area that was the most impaired in the last couple of years in terms of market and liquidity.

Finally, 2025 was a reasonably strong year for realizations. Those were up 47%. Our realizations were most commonly from our pre-2022 vintages... These $26 billion in realizations were driven primarily by direct assets, which accounted for 73% of the mix. Notably, direct equity realizations were up 54% year-over-year

Q4 Guidance Call

Another thing to applaud is that these realisations were at premiums to asset values meaning they've been conservative so far.

In terms of investment, there are still struggles in driving new capital into new ideas, and equity is not as featured here but they are in a better position with capital being realised sooner to compete effectively for quality deals, which are likely the ones seeing liquidity first as buyers and sellers settle on prices.

Nonetheless, realisations are expected to bring performance fees back to between 25-40% of revenues. They've already reached the lower end of the range as of the latest more comprehensive interim disclosure.

Bottom Line

We think we can assess the value situation with a basic observation that if they reach 40% of revenue from performance fee, their net profit should increase by around another 20%, in line with the 20% increase YoY of 20% as of the interim reports. A forward PE of 21.3x based on analyst consensus seems reasonable when compressing the FY 2024 multiple by a little over 20% from both management and performance fee growth. That puts them at being a little more expensive than Bridgepoint Group and CVC (CVCCF) at below 20x each. There's no relative angle at least. CVC for example is also realising quite well. Therefore we don't feel that Partners Group is that much of a steal at this point.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Fed Takes A Wait-And-See Approach
data.description:
By Bryan Cutsinger

Labor markets are stabilizing and inflation has leveled off. Further easing would’ve risked recent progress toward price stability.

The Federal Reserve held its target range for the federal funds rate constant in January 2026 at 3.5–3.75 percent. This decision was consistent with market expectations for the path of the federal funds rate, which for weeks had indicated that the Fed would hold rates steady at its January meeting. It is also consistent with rates prescribed by leading monetary policy rules. Notably, Governors Stephen Miran and Christopher Waller dissented from the decision, with both favoring a 25-basis point cut.

At the post-meeting press conference, Powell pointed to elevated inflation and a stabilizing labor market to explain the Fed’s decision to hold rates steady. He said Fed officials now “see the current stance of monetary policy as appropriate to promote progress” toward both sides of the dual mandate. Previously, Fed officials had expressed concern about the tensions facing the Fed’s dual mandate amid a softening labor market. Powell said that available data show “economic activity has been expanding at a solid pace,” driven primarily by consumer spending and business fixed investment. He acknowledged the lingering effects of last fall’s prolonged government shutdown but suggested that any drag on activity in the third and fourth quarters of last year will likely be reversed in the first quarter of 2026.

After softening for much of last year, labor market conditions now appear to be stabilizing, Powell explained. He pointed to relatively low and stable unemployment in recent months as evidence that the labor market may be at or near maximum employment. Echoing past statements, Powell acknowledged that the slowing pace of job growth likely reflects changes in both labor supply and labor demand. He said other indicators — such as job openings, layoffs, hiring, and nominal wage growth — “show little change in recent months.”

Powell acknowledged that inflation has remained stubbornly above the Fed’s two-percent target, with PCE inflation likely coming in at 2.9 percent over the 12 months from December 2024 to December 2025. Elevated inflation, he contended, “largely reflects inflation in the goods sector, which has been boosted by the effects of tariffs.” At the same time, Powell emphasized that longer-run inflation expectations remain aligned with the Fed’s two-percent target. Taken together, these claims suggest that inflation remains a concern for Fed officials, but one that is driven primarily by temporary, non-monetary forces.

According to Powell, the current target range for the federal funds rate is “within a range of plausible estimates of neutral” — that is, consistent with neither an overly accommodative nor restrictive stance of monetary policy. Holding rates steady, Powell argued, should help stabilize the labor market while allowing inflation to return to target “once the effects of tariff increases have passed through” to the price level.

By attributing elevated inflation primarily to tariff-driven increases in goods prices, the Fed is implicitly treating today’s inflation as a transitory relative-price adjustment rather than a broader monetary phenomenon. If that diagnosis is correct, a wait-and-see approach may be appropriate. There are, however, reasons to be skeptical.

Total dollar spending in the economy rose sharply relative to expectations in the third quarter of 2025, a pattern that is difficult to reconcile with a genuinely neutral stance of monetary policy. When nominal spending accelerates at this pace, it suggests that monetary conditions remain accommodative, regardless of how inflation is distributed across sectors.

More troubling is the fact that, despite the surge in dollar spending last year, financial markets are currently projecting two additional 25-basis point cuts to the federal funds rate over the coming year. Given that inflation is still running above target, it is difficult to see which economic conditions would warrant further monetary easing. Absent a clear deterioration in real activity or a decisive return of inflation to target, additional rate cuts risk reinforcing the very spending pressures the Fed is attempting to contain.

Ultimately, the Fed’s current posture reflects a high degree of confidence that inflationary pressures will fade without further policy restraint. That confidence rests on the view that inflation is largely the result of temporary, tariff-driven distortions rather than excess nominal demand. But if that view proves mistaken, the cost of waiting — and especially of easing further — could be a renewed loss of progress toward price stability. For a central bank whose credibility depends on keeping expectations firmly anchored, misdiagnosing the source of inflation is not a neutral error. It is an error that compounds over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 8% High Dividend Yield From Annaly Capital Preferred Share
data.description:
Many investors like the idea of high yields in the mortgage REIT space but don’t like all the risk. We have a great option for them - preferred shares. Preferred shares in the mREIT sector have relatively high dividend yields but do not carry the same risk as the common stock.

Annaly Capital (NLY) Preferred Share

NLY-G (NLY.PR.G) is an interesting preferred share because it has a lower floating rate than any of the other preferred shares we cover.

This allows it to trade at a lower price, and some investors will really appreciate the fact that the yield to call (14.1%) looks better for NLY-G than for any other preferred share with a risk rating of 1.

The downside to NLY-G is that the yield to call is unlikely to ever be realized because it wouldn't make sense for Annaly Capital Management to call the preferred share under pretty much any realistic scenario.

NLY-G Scenarios

We can run a quick scenario analysis to see how easily that holds.

Scenario 1:

The first scenario is one in which short-term rates continue to rise. If we were to see short-term rates go up to seven percent, which is a dramatic departure from where they are currently and from the recent trend lower, it would push the dividend rate on this share dramatically higher. Investors would initially like the idea that the dividend rate is going so much higher. However, in that scenario, Annaly Capital Management still would not call the preferred share because there would not be a superior method of financing available. The preferred share would still have a fairly thin credit spread, and that credit spread is extremely relevant to their cost of capital.

Remember that if they don't have this preferred equity, they still need to get the financing from some other method. That could be repo financing or it could be baby bonds, but in the event that it was repo financing, it would be a very short-term source of financing that they had to roll over regularly. The appeal of NLY-G from the perspective of Annaly Capital Management is that it locks in their financing without susceptibility to being required to repay it at any point. They merely need to pay the dividend rate on preferred shares so they can maintain paying a common dividend.

Scenario 2:

If the short-term rate went substantially higher, then you as an investor would already be having other opportunities to invest your capital at much higher rates on bonds. So, for instance, if the short-term rate had gone to 7%, you would expect to see a similar rise in the one-year, two-year, and five-year rates, and you would expect to still see credit spreads available on other investments. Therefore, you would expect to have other opportunities to generate that same level of increased income that you would be getting from an NLY-G.

Therefore, we would not expect the price of NLY-G to increase substantially. It could certainly go modestly higher, but it probably wouldn't go significantly higher. For the most part, it would increase with dividend accrual, and it might increase by an extra 2% beyond that, or if shareholders are extremely lucky, then maybe they would get an extra 3% beyond dividend accrual. That is where I would see a soft limit on the share price. Any higher trade would probably be the result of a buyer who had a typo in their limit-buy price or used a market order.

Scenario 3:

If short-term rates were to fall, we get a worse scenario for investors in NLY-G because the dividend rate would continue to fall with the short-term rates, and since the credit spread is not particularly large, the reduction of short-term rates would represent an even larger percentage decrease in the dividend rate compared to shares that have a higher credit spread.

Scenario 4:

If we went back to having short-term rates near 0%, as we did for so many years since the Great Financial Crisis, it would be possible for the dividend rate on NLY-G to fall below 5%. That would be a fairly low level of return for an investor who is investing in preferred shares. They would expect to get at least slightly more than 5% with pretty much any of the other floating rate preferred shares.

If we use NLY-F (NLY.PR.F) as a comparison, we can see that the credit spread is materially wider.

The gap is not enormous, but it is still large enough to rank as material. 4.993% on NLY-F beats 4.172% on NLY-G by a solid margin.

I think most investors in NLY-G would be thoroughly disappointed if the dividend rate fell to less than 5%. And in that case, it wouldn't surprise me if the share price declined modestly to push the yield a little bit higher.

Risk Rating

We find NLY preferred shares to have the lowest risk in the mortgage REIT sector. Right behind NLY you have the preferred shares from Dynex Capital (DX) and AGNC Investment (AGNC). However, Annaly Capital has the only preferred shares that we have given a risk rating of 1. This makes them a great choice (when the price is right) for a conservative buy-and-hold investor.

If you're not familiar with the underlying company, then we can discuss it briefly. Annaly Capital Management is a mortgage REIT. The vast majority of their portfolio is agency mortgage-backed securities. The credit risk profile is extremely attractive because of the agency guarantee. However, it is worth considering that the agency guarantee could decrease in value if we see Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac exit their current relationship with the U.S. government. In my view, that would cause the securities to become a little bit more risky, particularly during periods of intense economic uncertainty. Today, because of the government's involvement in Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, there is an implicit guarantee which can help to prevent the spreads between agency MBS and treasury rates from reaching substantially higher levels.

data.title: Visa: A Must-Own Long Term, Just Not A Buy Today (Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

Visa Inc. (V) is hardly a company that needs an introduction. It's as ubiquitous as the global rails that let consumers, merchants, banks, and governments move money almost anywhere in the world. That kind of reach comes with real advantages, but it doesn’t make Visa immune to the cycle. I once wrote,

"Despite the fact that its scale offers it a significant edge over the majority of its rivals, it is nevertheless dependent on the same factors that drive spending everywhere else: how confident people feel to spend."

In that context, after going through the latest earnings report, I’m neutral for now. I think the fundamentals are fine, but the valuation's stretched. Add to that a fog hanging over the macro picture, which will probably keep a lid on any near-term upside.

Visa's Q1 Highlights: Volume, Pricing, and the Services Flywheel

Visa grew net revenue 15% to $10.9 billion. Payments volume rose 8% in constant dollars. Value-added services grew 28% in constant dollars to $3.2 billion, while commercial and money movement solutions were up 20%. Together, those two buckets drove about half of the total revenue growth. For now, consumer spending seems to be holding up. People are still swiping, tapping, and moving money, and Visa's right in the middle of it. Revenue growth-wise, pricing actions did their job, but lower currency volatility did weigh a bit.

In the prepared remarks, CFO Suh made a point of saying they’re not in the business of economic forecasting, which I think is only half true. Almost in the same breath, he still laid out a baseline view. The overall economic environment remains about the same, and consumer spending continues to stay firm.

So in this firm environment, service revenue got a boost from prior-quarter volume lagging through and higher card perks. At the same time, data processing benefited from pricing, more uptake of value-added services, and a higher cross-border mix. International transaction revenue was under pressure due to low market volatility, a shift toward lower-yield Visa Direct flows, and the impact of hedging. Other revenue jumped on advisory and marketing work tied to big events like the Olympics and FIFA.

What I really like is tokenization. There are now over 17.5 billion tokens in circulation. That’s more than 3x the number of physical cards. Because of that, authorization rates are higher, fraud is lower, and digital checkout just works better. At the same time, guest checkout on e-commerce websites now accounts for only 16% of purchases worldwide, and at the largest online sellers, it’s below 4%. Most shoppers, as you know, don’t really check out as guests anymore. Instead, buying's been boiled down to a few taps or a biometric step, which cuts out a lot of the friction in the process.

Moving on to commercial payments, volume grew 10%, which was faster than overall payments. This growth came from new successes in virtual payables, B2B card programs, and continued strong activity in cross-border payments. Visa Direct transactions increased by 23%, driven by higher payout activity in more volatile markets and by fintechs using it to scale their remittance businesses.

To add a splash of context to Visa Direct, CEO McInerney explained that the company's testing a new way for U.S. businesses to pay people by sending money straight to stablecoin wallets, making payouts faster and more reliable even in places with weak banks or unstable currencies. Overall, Visa says stablecoins are just one part of a bigger push to improve global money movement through Visa Direct and business products, emphasizing that “Stablecoin is just one way that we are enabling money movement.”

At the end of last year, I did a Visa vs. Mastercard (MA) write-up. Compared with Mastercard, Visa, to me, looks a step ahead on the services pivot. Mastercard’s value-added services have performed well, with growth ranging from about 18% to 23% in recent quarters, depending on the time period. Still, Visa’s VAS growth this quarter is stronger. More importantly, it makes up a bigger share of total revenue, which suggests Visa is monetizing these services more deeply, not just growing them. One of the core takeaways I argued was that Mastercard puts a strong focus on advisory, data, and risk services, while Visa gets more added benefit from its broader technology stack, like processing, tokens, credentials, and money movement, which makes it less dependent on payment volume growth alone.

You can see that this hyperscaler angle really tightens this edge. Partners build directly on Visa's credentials. Currently, there are 5 billion of them, along with their tokens and platforms like Visa Intelligent Commerce. So instead of just running the network, the company turns it into infrastructure that other players scale on. On top of that, agentic commerce is already in pilot with more than 100 partners. Stablecoin settlement volume is running at about a $4.6 billion annualized pace. And Flex Credentials are up to roughly 20 million and still growing. Not exactly side projects. Over time, they build on the core and compound the move toward services-led growth.

And lastly, guidance. According to the CFO:

...assuming the macroeconomic environment stays generally where it has been and consumer spending remains resilient. So no change. On pricing, we also have no material changes with the benefits of new pricing expected to be similar in magnitude as last year and the majority in the back half.

In simple terms, Q2 should see less year-over-year pricing benefit than Q1. Incentive growth is expected to rise from Q1 to Q2, peak in Q3, and leave the full-year outlook largely unchanged.

Quality Business, Tighter Multiple

Visa's currently priced at ~28x its earnings, according to FAST Graphs.

This price reflects a mix of the company’s past earnings growth of 20.11% and expectations that EPS will grow 11.7% in fiscal 2026, rising to 13.07% by 2029.

So even at this level, Visa's valuation is still below its five-year average forward P/E of ~30, a bit more caution than usual, probably stemming from regulatory concerns, including those possible limits on card fees under the Credit Card Competition Act.

Even so, it goes hand in hand with Visa’s strong and hard-to-replicate payment network and its growing focus on higher-value services, which now account for about half of Visa’s revenue growth (driven by things like payment tokens and agentic commerce). At the same time, competition from stablecoins and buy-now-pay-later providers creates execution risk. Risk-wise, if Visa struggles to scale these newer services, it could put pressure on its long-term growth rate of more than 10% per year.

An economic slowdown could also hurt Visa by reducing how much consumers spend, especially if higher-income customers think twice about living it up, and business spending doesn’t make up the difference. But we have a nice cushion with that $21.1 billion in annual buybacks. However, the projected annual return is -9.59%, which indicates the stock is very much overvalued compared with its estimated FV of $178.75. And with shares trading at 25.93x forward earnings, clearer regulatory outcomes will need to arrive soon to justify the valuation.

Final Takeaway

I'm downgrading Visa to a Hold here. As I'm writing this, the market doesn't care too much about this recent update. I get selling, and near-term, historically speaking, there are a couple of scattered Sell ratings here and there, but I can't imagine ever really seeing a time when I'd be pressed to issue one myself. Overall, Visa's a must-have in a portfolio, one that I've owned for years.

In terms of near-term momentum, Visa's robust 15% revenue growth and VAS/commercial momentum do signal strong upside from digital innovations, but if you're looking to buy in now, considering this overvaluation, I'd wait for a stronger pullback. As for aggressive, growth-oriented investors with high risk tolerance, you should at least buy a starter position for a long-term moat.

data.title: Can Semiconductor Stocks Keep Delivering?
data.description:
Semiconductor stocks have helped push markets to record highs, but volatility remains a risk. Andrew McKendry, VP and Director for Portfolio Research with TD Asset Management joins MoneyTalk to discuss how chip stocks can live up to the potential for AI profitability.

Transcript

Andrew McKendry:The overarching message, I think, I'd like to leave your viewers is really simple, and that is that AI usage is driving a surge in semiconductor demand, and we simply cannot make enough chips. Let's talk a bit about where we came from before getting into the current events.

So I've got a chart here looking at 2024 through August 2025. And what I find interesting here is, when we were talking about semis, what we were really referring to was one stock, NVIDIA (NVDA). So that-- up fourfold during that time. The semiconductor index, the broad collection of stocks, rose but was really just in line with the S&P.

So, if we now take a look at August and going forward, we see a striking reversal here. Now NVIDIA has stopped outperforming. It's working alongside S&P. But the green line here, the rapid uptick in this broader semi index, is very much a big change from what we've seen before and something we could talk about today a little more.

Greg Bonnell: Yeah, let's start digging in there. First, we'll start with NVIDIA and why it has stalled. Is it simple a case that just couldn't go on that path forever?

Andrew McKendry: Yeah, it's a good question. I think there's a few things. I would say, size, share, and skeptics. So, what do I mean here? Size-- so NVIDIA-- once you reach a market cap of 4-5 trillion, I think it becomes a little tougher for investors to digest the growth outlook. Still very solid, but very much tougher than to see an NVIDIA, say, doubling, tripling from there than a smaller market cap.

The other piece is market share. So we know there are other participants in the market. NVIDIA still has 90% share in this accelerator market. But Broadcom (AVGO), through their Google work, is a real participant now. AMD is coming up with a very viable competitive product to NVIDIA that's going to come out this year. And so, when you get this dynamic of market share shift that comes into the narrative, that's another area that could weigh on the stock.

And then third is the skeptics. A lot of talk around AI bubbles. We've had high-profile investor commentary out there talking about NVIDIA and really just questioning the growth outlook altogether. And so I think those things combined have slowed the outperformance, which has been quite remarkable, actually, the last couple of years.

Greg Bonnell: So that's the NVIDIA story. But as your charts are showing us, it's more than just about NVIDIA now. Let's talk about what's going on beyond that name.

Andrew McKendry: Sure. So I've got another chart here breaking down the semiconductor index and some of the non-NVIDIA performers. The top stock there, the top line there in blue, is Micron (MU). Micron produces memory, and memory has really just been on fire.

Interesting dynamic here. This is a subsector that has traditionally been what I would perceive as lower-quality over a prolonged period of time. Doesn't generate a lot of free cash flow. Pricing tends to go through boom/bust cycles. But here, as we sit here today, memory prices have literally gone off the charts-- in some cases, triple what a maximum price would have been in the past. And this ties back to AI as well.

So, we've seen-- I think the memory producers in aggregate were caught a little bit flat-footed, I think, in terms of capacity increases. And the reason is this real surge in AI demand that I mentioned, the inferencing side, which is really just the usage-- so if you think of the Sora video generation product that people are using, and other such applications have driven a substantial increase in demand. And so, there's questions here around sustainability. But of course, as long as prices are off the charts, so can the stock be.

Greg Bonnell: Has there been an underinvestment in capacity, considering all this big boom in demand we had for AI and we're hearing with these shortages? Have they just not put the money into the space?

Andrew McKendry: You're right. There's been an underinvestment in capacity. And that actually ties into one of the other names on the chart that we see-- is ASML. This might be a lesser-known stock, perhaps, but it's a European champion. They essentially make equipment to produce semiconductors, these vast, multi-hundred-million-dollar machines that essentially look like printing presses, I suppose, to print the chips.

And to your point, this specific subsector of semiconductors, which is the semi-cap equipment side, has been underinvested. Part of it is the memory. They've not been adding capacity. Part of it is the logic side. And Taiwan, TSMC (TSM), has not been dramatically increasing capacity. And so, the way I think about it here on equipment is, we're approaching-- for the last number of years, we've spent about a hundred billion a year as an industry on equipment. But now, with semi sales quickly approaching a trillion dollars, I think that number could be easily 50% higher, and we're starting to see that come into the share prices as well.

Greg Bonnell: Okay. So, that's a nice overview of where we were, where we are right now. Where could things go from here? Some people will start saying that perhaps the best days are behind the space.

Andrew McKendry: Yeah, good question. Well, I firmly believe that the AI trade is still intact, but we are seeing a broadening-out. It seems to be driving the space overall.

And just across the semiconductor ecosystem, we're just seeing a ramp-up in spend.

Another data point is hyperscalers. So, I know we just heard from Meta (META) last night, raising the CapEx. And I think their numbers-- they might be growing 70% this year. And so we're starting to hear about real ROI, real use cases that have pushed back on the AI bubble narrative, so.

Greg Bonnell: When you think about-- it's been about a year since DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), right?

Andrew McKendry: Yeah.

Greg Bonnell: What lessons did we learn from that? Because that really rattled the market, and now nobody talks-- we've moved on from it.

Andrew McKendry: Yeah, that was a painful time to be, actually, covering the semiconductor space because it just introduced a lot of debate as to whether all of this investment is worthwhile.
